narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 52% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to flag higher-risk applicants. Your profile includes being in the 4th decile for grades in both your first and third years of law school—lower than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile—along with a middle-class family income, an LSAT score of 22.5 well below the average of 36.7, an undergraduate GPA of 2.9 compared to the usual 3.21, identifying as female and Black, and planning full-time studies like most applicants. These details paint a picture of solid determination but academic performance that's consistently below what the model sees in students who typically pass the bar. The biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your third-year decile rank, as it stood out as the strongest influence signaling potential struggles ahead. Right behind that was your first-year decile, reinforcing a pattern of middling performance relative to peers. Your family income level came next, slightly tilting the odds downward compared to higher-earning backgrounds that often correlate with bar success. Your LSAT score had a notable pull too, given how far it lags behind the norm and what successful students usually score. Your racial background as Black followed closely, reflecting underrepresented groups' tougher historical odds in this dataset. Your undergraduate GPA nudged things further toward risk, being under the average that predicts stronger bar outcomes. Gender and full-time status had smaller but still unfavorable influences here. Overall, every aspect of your profile contributed to raising the failure risk, with no standout strengths to counterbalance them—unlike typical approved students who shine in at least a couple of these areas. To boost your future bar chances, focus on what you can realistically improve, like retaking the LSAT for a higher score or adopting study strategies to lift your law school deciles in remaining years. Building those academic markers could shift your profile closer to passers' averages and open new doors. Hang in there—you've got the drive, and targeted improvements can make a real difference.
